title: Basic Words
description: The 30 most-used words in everyday Delhi Hindi. No formal textbook words here.
culturalNote: Hindi and English mix freely in Delhi — don't feel weird about it. 'Bahut stress hai' is perfectly normal. Young Delhiites switch between Hindi and English mid-sentence all the time. You'll fit right in.

exercises.note: Tone matters everything. Drawn-out 'Haaan' = obviously yes. Quick 'Haan' = neutral yes. 'Ha' or just 'Hmm' = super casual agreement. Also written 'Han'.
Softened with 'na' at the end: 'nahi na' sounds gentler than just 'nahi'. Quick 'Na' alone is also very common — like a soft no.
'Bas' is incredibly versatile. 'Bas yahi chahiye' (just this is needed), 'Bas kar!' (stop it!), 'Bas 5 minute' (just 5 minutes), 'Bas theek hai' (that's enough, it's fine).

exercises.prompt: Your friend keeps teasing you and won't stop. What do you say?

exercises.note: 'Acha?' rising tone = 'Really?!' | 'Acha.' flat = 'okay, got it' | 'Acha acha' = 'yeah yeah I know' (sometimes dismissive) | 'Bahut acha' = very good. One of the most-used words in Hindi.
Your all-purpose agreement and sign-off. 'Theek hai yaar' closes almost any plan. 'Theek?' alone = 'alright?' (checking in).

exercises.sentenceEnglish: Plan for Sunday? _____ dude, let's do it.

exercises.english: Let's go / Okay then / Come on / Move
exercises.note: 'Chal' starts AND ends conversations in Delhi. 'Chal, bye!' is the classic Delhi goodbye. 'Chal na' = come on (urging someone gently). 'Chal yaar' = let's do it, man.

exercises.instruction: Match each Hindi word to its meaning

exercises.note: Delhi's most essential word. Add it to almost anything to sound natural: 'yaar kya hua?' / 'theek hai yaar' / 'chal yaar'. Not gender-specific — women use it just as freely as men.
'Arre' expresses surprise, frustration, or emphasis depending on tone. 'Arre yaar!' = oh man! | 'Arre haan!' = oh yes of course! | 'Arre nahi!' = no way! | 'Arre chal' = oh come on.

exercises.prompt: Your friend tells you something really surprising. You want to express 'Oh wow, no way!' What word do you start with?
exercises.hint: It's an exclamation of surprise or disbelief

exercises.spanish: O sea / Es decir / O sea que
exercises.note: 'Matlab' works exactly like 'I mean' or 'like' as an English filler. 'Matlab woh aaya hi nahi' = I mean, he just didn't show up. Very Delhi. You'll hear it constantly.

exercises.spanish: Por cierto / De todas formas / En realidad
exercises.note: 'Waise bhi' = anyway / besides (adding emphasis). 'Waise' at the start = 'by the way'. 'Waise toh sab theek hai' = otherwise everything's fine.

exercises.instruction: Match the filler word to its closest English equivalent

exercises.note: 'Abhi' = right now. 'Abhi nahi' = not right now. 'Abhi aa rahi hoon' = I'm coming right now (feminine form -rahi). 'Abhi abhi' = just this moment.

exercises.english: Yesterday OR Tomorrow (context tells you which!)
exercises.note: Warning: same word, opposite times! 'Kal hua' (past tense) = it happened yesterday. 'Kal hoga' (future tense) = it will happen tomorrow. Context + verb tense = key.

exercises.prompt: 'Kal milte hain' — what does this mean?
exercises.hint: 'Milte hain' points to the future — and kal + future = ?

exercises.note: Simple and unambiguous — just today! 'Aaj kya plan hai?' = what's the plan today? 'Aaj nahi' = not today.
'Phir milte hain' = we'll meet later/again. 'Phir bhi' = even so / still. 'Phir?' = then what? / so what happened? 'Phir se' = once again.
Literally 'path/address not known'. Extremely common. 'Pata nahi yaar' is a complete, socially acceptable answer to almost any awkward question. Also: 'mujhe pata nahi' = more formal version.
Stronger than 'haan'. 'Bilkul sahi' = absolutely right. 'Bilkul nahi' = absolutely not. 'Bilkul' alone as a response = 100% yes, definitely.
'Bahut acha' = very good. 'Bahut ho gaya' = that's way too much now. 'Bahut' is your main intensifier — use it everywhere. 'Bahut zyada' = way too much.
'Thoda ruko' = wait a bit. 'Thoda thoda' = little by little / bit by bit. 'Thoda sa' = just a tiny bit. The gentler alternative to bahut.

exercises.prompt: You want to say 'that's way too much!' What word do you use?
How do you ask 'why?' in Hindi?
How do you ask 'how?' in Hindi?
